ARC60304 / ARC61604 — Assignment 1

The Wall
That Works

A research study into kinetic facade systems, adaptive mechanisms, wind energy generation, and the evolution toward a self-powered responsive building skin.

Scroll
01

What is a Kinetic Facade?

"A kinetic facade is a dynamic building envelope that responds to environmental stimuli — wind, sun, temperature, or occupant behaviour — through controlled movement of its surface components."
Purpose

Environmental Adaptation

Unlike static facades, kinetic systems continuously adjust to changing external conditions, optimising thermal comfort, daylight quality, and energy consumption without mechanical HVAC reliance.

Mechanism

Responsive Movement

Panels, fins, louvres, or membranes pivot, rotate, or fold in response to sensors or natural forces. Movement can be actuator-driven, wind-driven, or shape-memory material-driven.

Significance

Active Sustainability

Kinetic facades represent the shift from passive to active building skins — where the envelope itself becomes an energy management system rather than a static boundary.

02

Types of
Kinetic Systems

Kinetic facades are classified by their actuation method — how and why they move.

01

Wind-Driven Passive

Panels respond purely to wind force with no electronic components. Natural air movement causes rotation or oscillation, providing self-regulating ventilation and shading without energy input.

Passive
02

Actuator-Driven Active

Motorised panels controlled by sensors and building management systems. Precisely programmable but requires electrical power and maintenance for servo motors and control hardware.

Active
03

Solar-Tracking

Panels orient themselves to follow the sun's path throughout the day. Used for both shading control and optimising photovoltaic energy generation on building surfaces.

Active
04

Shape-Memory / Thermobimetal

Materials that physically deform in response to temperature changes. No motors required — the material itself acts as both sensor and actuator, curling or expanding with heat.

Passive
05

Turbine-Integrated Energy-Harvesting

Facade elements double as wind turbine rotors, converting kinetic wind energy into electricity. The movement that regulates light and heat also generates power for the building.

Hybrid
06

Pneumatic / Membrane

Inflatable ETFE cushions or tensile membranes that change opacity, insulation, and form by adjusting air pressure. Common in large-span structures requiring lightweight adaptive coverage.

Active
03

Precedent
Studies

Five key precedents — three kinetic facade systems and two wind energy-harvesting buildings — that inform the design direction.

Kinetic Facade Precedents — 01 / 03
Abu Dhabi, UAE — 2012

Al Bahar Towers

Aedas Architects — Abu Dhabi, UAE

Twin 25-storey towers wrapped in a computer-controlled mashrabiya screen. The 2,090 triangular umbrella-like panels open and close in response to sun position throughout the day, reducing solar gain by approximately 50% while maintaining views and daylight.

Mechanism Analysis

Each GRP (glass-reinforced polymer) panel is attached to an aluminium frame actuated by a linear servo motor. A building management system calculates sun position in real time and sends individual commands to each panel. Panels take 3 minutes to fully open or close.

Strengths
  • 50% solar heat gain reduction
  • Culturally responsive geometry
  • Individually addressable panels
  • Proven at high-rise scale
Limitations
  • Requires constant power for motors
  • High maintenance cost
  • No energy generation
  • Wind loading challenges at height
Brisbane, Australia — 2008

Brisbane Airport Parking Garage

Cox Rayner Architects — Brisbane, Australia

A multi-storey car park clad with 220,000 wind-responsive aluminium panels. The panels move freely with ambient wind, creating a constantly shifting pattern across the facade while naturally ventilating the interior. No motors, no sensors, no power.

Mechanism Analysis

Each diamond-shaped panel is mounted on a central pivot point with a slight off-centre weight. Wind pressure causes the panels to rotate around their pivot. The off-centre mass ensures panels return to a resting angle when wind ceases. Entirely passive — zero energy input required.

Strengths
  • Fully passive — zero energy needed
  • 220,000 panels, minimal maintenance
  • Natural ventilation enhancement
  • Low construction complexity
Limitations
  • No energy generation capability
  • No programmable response
  • Limited daylight control precision
  • Aesthetic-dominant, not performance-driven
Various Locations — 2000s–Present

Wind Veil

Ned Kahn — Multiple International Installations

Thousands of small anodised aluminium tiles suspended on pins, each independently responding to air movement. The installation makes wind patterns visible across the building surface, creating a continuously changing visual phenomenon. Used on carparks, terminals, and towers globally.

Mechanism Analysis

Individual perforated square tiles are suspended from a single top pin, hanging freely like pendulums. Air turbulence causes each tile to flutter independently. The perforations reduce wind load while maintaining reflective visual effect. Assembly is modular — panels clip onto a standard subframe.

Strengths
  • Extremely lightweight system
  • Modular and scalable
  • Zero mechanical components
  • Strong visual identity
Limitations
  • Purely aesthetic — no energy output
  • No shading or daylight control
  • Random movement, not programmable
  • Limited environmental performance
Wind Turbine Energy Precedents — 02 / 02
Manama, Bahrain — 2008

Bahrain World Trade Center

Atkins — Manama, Bahrain

The world's first skyscraper to integrate large-scale wind turbines as part of its structural design. Three horizontal-axis turbines (29m diameter each) span between the twin sail-shaped towers, harvesting wind channelled and accelerated by the building's aerodynamic form.

Mechanism & Energy Generation

The twin towers are shaped as aerofoils that funnel prevailing sea breezes through the turbine bridges. Each turbine uses a horizontal-axis design with three blades. The turbines generate an estimated 1,100–1,300 MWh/year, supplying approximately 11–15% of the towers' energy demand. The building orientation was determined by Bahrain's dominant wind direction.

Strengths
  • Proven building-integrated wind power
  • Architecture shaped to enhance wind flow
  • Large-scale energy contribution
  • Iconic precedent for wind + architecture
Limitations
  • Requires high & consistent wind speeds
  • Turbines separated from the facade
  • Massive scale — not facade-integrated
  • Vibration & noise management challenges
Savonius Rotor — Invented 1922 by S.J. Savonius

Savonius Rotor Vertical-Axis Wind Turbines

Applied Research — Various Facade Integration Studies

The Savonius rotor is a vertical-axis wind turbine using two or more scooped half-cylinder blades arranged in an S-shape. Unlike horizontal-axis turbines, it operates at low wind speeds (as low as 1–2 m/s), accepts wind from any direction, and produces low noise — making it ideal for urban and facade-integrated applications.

Mechanism & Energy Generation

Wind pressure acts differently on the concave versus convex face of each blade, creating a net rotational torque. The drag-based principle means it starts rotating at very low wind speeds. A central vertical shaft connects to a small generator or alternator. Electricity output is DC, converted via inverter. Research installations report 50–200W per unit at average urban wind speeds of 3–5 m/s.

Strengths
  • Operates from 1 m/s wind speed
  • Omnidirectional — no yaw needed
  • Low noise, low vibration
  • Compact — integrable at panel scale
  • Simple construction, few moving parts
Limitations
  • Lower efficiency than HAWT (~15–25%)
  • Output limited by rotor size
  • Aggregate output from many units needed
  • Structural load at facade must be managed
04

Design
Criteria

Derived from the collective pros and cons of all five precedents — the non-negotiables for the final design to succeed where others fell short.

C01

Low Wind Speed Operation

The system must activate at wind speeds of 1–3 m/s to be viable in low-wind urban environments like Kuala Lumpur. High-speed dependency (as seen in Bahrain WTC) is not suitable for Malaysian conditions.

← From: Bahrain WTC limitation + Savonius strength
C02

Passive-First, Zero Standby Power

The facade must function without consuming electricity for its own operation. Unlike Al Bahar's motor-dependent system, movement must be wind-driven and self-sustaining, only outputting power — never consuming it.

← From: Al Bahar limitation + Brisbane Airport strength
C03

Dual Function: Shading + Energy

Every panel must perform both environmental control (daylight modulation, heat gain reduction) and energy generation simultaneously. Single-purpose panels (Ned Kahn — aesthetic only) are insufficient.

← From: Ned Kahn limitation + design intent
C04

Lightweight Construction ≤ 5 kg/m²

Panel weight must be manageable for facade-mounting without requiring heavy substructure upgrades. Aluminium sheet, perforated skin, and minimal generator components must keep the assembly below 5 kg per square metre.

← From: Wind Veil + Ned Kahn lightweight approach
C05

Modular and Scalable

The panel unit must be repeatable across the entire facade without custom engineering per panel. A single panel design must tile seamlessly at any facade scale, from a small office to a high-rise tower.

← From: Brisbane Airport + Ned Kahn modular systems
C06

Omnidirectional Wind Response

The turbine geometry must accept wind from any horizontal direction. Vertical-axis Savonius configuration achieves this inherently — the system must not require wind from a fixed direction to function.

← From: Savonius VAWT strength vs. HAWT limitation
05

Design
Direction

The strategic synthesis — combining kinetic facade principles with Savonius turbine technology into a single integrated facade panel system.

Kinetic Facade + Wind Turbine Integration

The design direction merges two previously separate architectural strategies: the responsive kinetic facade (which controls light and heat) and the building-integrated wind turbine (which generates electricity). The innovation is making them the same object.

Rather than adding turbines to a building that already has a facade, or adding shading to a building that already has turbines — the panel itself becomes the turbine, the shading device, and the daylight modulator simultaneously.

The Savonius rotor geometry, scaled to the size of an individual facade panel, creates an S-blade form that rotates vertically. At slow rotation, it admits diffused light. At high rotation (strong wind), it generates more electricity while reducing solar penetration. At rest, it acts as a fixed louvre.

Savonius VAWT Facade Scale Wind-Driven Passive First Dual Function Lightweight Aluminium Urban Context
Design Logic Flow
01

Problem Identified

Conventional facades are passive — they admit heat, waste wind energy, and cannot adapt to changing environmental conditions.

↓
02

Kinetic Facade Principle

Moving panels can control solar heat gain, daylight, and ventilation. Proven by Al Bahar and Brisbane Airport precedents.

↓
03

Wind Turbine Integration

If the panel must move anyway, that movement can drive a generator. Savonius VAWT geometry works at panel scale and low wind speeds.

↓
04

One Change

Replace the static facade panel with a Savonius turbine panel — the building skin simultaneously shades, controls light, and generates electricity.

06

Original
Concept

The Savonius wind turbine facade panel — the single architectural change that transforms a passive building skin into a self-powered adaptive system.

Savonius rotor geometry — S-blade cross-section
01

Wind Hits Blade

Ambient wind exerts unequal pressure on concave vs convex blade face

02

Blade Rotates

Net torque causes vertical-axis rotation from wind speeds as low as 1 m/s

03

Power Generated

Central shaft drives a compact generator — DC electricity stored or fed to grid

The Savonius Turbine Facade Panel

The concept replaces a conventional flat facade panel with a Savonius-geometry rotor panel. Each unit is a double-blade S-curve form fabricated from perforated 1.5mm aluminium sheet, mounted on a vertical axis with a micro-generator at the hinge point.

The blade rotation is driven entirely by wind — no motors, no sensors, no power consumption. The rotating panel naturally modulates how much direct sunlight enters the interior: slow rotation diffuses light, fast rotation blocks direct glare, and the resting angle acts as a fixed louvre.

Across a full facade, hundreds of these panels operate independently, each responding to localised wind and creating a constantly shifting, living surface — while collectively generating measurable electricity.

Panel Type
Savonius Vertical-Axis Wind Turbine
Material
1.5mm perforated aluminium sheet, anodised
Target Weight
~3.2 kg/m² total panel assembly
Min. Wind Speed
1–2 m/s (suitable for KL urban context)
Actuation
Fully passive — wind-driven only
Energy Output
50–150W per unit at 3–5 m/s wind
Daylight Control
Variable — rotation angle governs solar penetration
Concept Origin
S.J. Savonius, 1922 — scaled to facade panel unit
Design Statement

"The kinetic facade reimagines the conventional building skin as an active, responsive system. By integrating the Savonius wind turbine principle into individual facade panels, a single architectural element transforms into a passive energy harvester, daylight modulator, and climate buffer simultaneously. The wall that works."

Taylor's University
School of Architecture, Building & Design — MASSA
